name: partner in creation
shortDescription: A conceptual deity representing the female principle in the creation of the world and other deities in Shinto mythology. This entity is often associated with Izanami, the female counterpart to Izanagi in the creation myth.
description: The 'partner in creation' is a conceptual entity within Japanese mythology, representing the feminine principle and vital force necessary for creation and procreation. This entity is intrinsically linked to the deity Izanami, who embodies the female aspect of creation alongside her male counterpart, Izanagi. Together, they are responsible for birthing the islands of Japan and many of the Shinto deities. The story of their creation begins with a divine command to solidify the floating land and create the islands. Standing on the Floating Bridge of Heaven, they stirred the ocean with the jeweled spear Amenonuhoko, and when they lifted the spear, the brine that dripped from its tip formed the island of Onogoro-shima. They descended to this island and built a palace and a pillar called the Amenomihashira, around which they performed a ritual to solidify their union. This union resulted in the birth of various deities, though some were born imperfectly. Their most significant creation was the islands of Japan, including Awaji, Shikoku, Oki, Kyushu, Iki, Tsushima, and Sado. The ‘partner in creation’ concept highlights the essential role of the feminine in the Shinto creation narrative, emphasizing balance and interdependence between male and female energies in bringing forth the world and its divine inhabitants. The tragedy of Izanami's death during the birth of Kagutsuchi, the fire deity, and Izanagi's subsequent descent into Yomi (the underworld) and purification ritual, further underscores the profound impact of the feminine principle on the cycle of life and death in Japanese mythology.
